EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the region from the 3rd to the 5th of January. This produced between 1 to 2 feet of snow in the Tahoe Basin with 2 to 4 feet for the higher elevations. This system also produced winds upwards of 90 mph on the ridges, with gusts commonly upwards of 55 mph elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station SSYC1, Surprise Valley recorded a wind gust of 54 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: June Mountain Ski Base reported 40 inches of snow, Mammoth Mountain reported 26 inches, CoCoRaHs observer measured 8.5 inches, and trained spotter 1 SW Toms Place measured 6.5 inches. These snow reports are storm totals.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of snowmobilers triggered an avalanche near Johnson Peak and Castle Peak north of Truckee. One snowmobiler was fully buried and killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive long lasting period of foggy conditions prevailed across the San Joaquin Valley from January 12th through January 31st (It continued through February 7th). Dense fog coverage increased for the next several days each morning starting on the 11th. A localized area with near zero visibility was reported south of Fresno on the morning (around 9:15 AM) of the 11th that unfortunately resulted in a multi-vehicle collision that caused quite a few injuries and one fatality. Fog continued to develop each night and morning for at least another week afterward with some locations remaining foggy for the entire day during the 16th until the 19th, or only a brief improvement in visibility during the afternoon hours.||On the 20th and 21st, most locations in the Central Valley continued to observe fog during the nighttime and morning hours, but was not as widespread. Also, valley fog onset was largely later in the evening on the 20th. The fog pattern continued through the end of the month, except most locations in the San Joaquin Valley did not observe dense fog on the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th due to an upper-level disturbance (with the exception of Merced). The fog continued through the end of the month. However, more major accidents due to fog happened on the 27th and 31st with numerous injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KHJO Hanford Airport reported 0.25 mile visibility.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought mountain snow and valley rain to much of Inyo County, causing flood impacts in Death Valley and surrounding areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event snow totals ranged between 15 and 20 inches at several SNOTELs in the Eastern Sierra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A traffic collision occurred at 7:26 pm on January 20th 2026 in the south bound lanes of Highway 70 by Striplin Road in Sutter County, California. Patchy dense fog was present during the crash. The event was a wrong way driver on the southbound lane of Highway 70. There were two injuries and one fatality from this incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traffic collision occurred at 7:26 pm on January 20th 2026 in the south bound lanes of Highway 70 by Striplin Road in Sutter County, California. Dense fog was present in Sutter County California on Highway 70, with visibilities of a quarter mile or less. Two cars where involved in the crash, with a woman and an infant with major injuries and a fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the southwest tapped into anomalously moist tropical air as it pushed into Southern California on New Year's Day. This allowed for some heavy rainfall rates and flooding, especially near the San Diego Metro. There were several reports of flooding along the San Diego River. Showers continued off and on in the following days, but impacts were limited after New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closure on Southbound Hwy 1 from Warner Ave to Seapoint St due to flood control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong Santa Ana winds set up late in the day on January 8th along coastal slopes and into the valleys of Southern California in the wake of an inside slider low pressure system. The pattern remained favorable for Santa Ana winds through the weekend with strong surface high pressure in place over the intermountain west. Gusts of 60-80+ mph were common along coastal slopes and through the foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS station recorded a 76 mph wind gust at Fremont Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with storm surge based off westerly swell and strong winds contributed to major coastal flooding impacts during the first few days of January. Impacts were seen across the coastal regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, including locations of the Pacific coast that were typically insulated from the impacts of tidal flooding. A Coastal Flood Advisory was put in place starting December 31 through January 5, initially for the San Francisco and Monterey Bays but eventually including the Pacific Coast. A Coastal Flood Warning was put in place for January 2 and 3 for the San Francisco and San Pablo Bayshore areas. The San Francisco tide gauge reached 8.40 feet above MLLW on January 3, which would make it the fourth highest water level at the station on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding was reported on the Seminary onramp to southbound US Highway 101.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms impacted the Bay Area and Central Coast from January 1st to the 5th, causing numerous flooding impacts and scattered thunderstorms. A low pressure system on the 1st was followed by a strong cold front and associated atmospheric river late on the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd.  A second cold front followed on the 4th. A tornado warning was issued on January 5 for the Pacific coast near the border between San Mateo and Santa Cruz counties due to a radar indicated rotation signature. Around 3,400 customers in San Francisco lost power on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pine Mountain Fire Road reported a gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with storm surge based off westerly swell and strong winds contributed to major coastal flooding impacts during the first few days of January. Impacts were seen across the coastal regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, including locations of the Pacific coast that were typically insulated from the impacts of tidal flooding. A Coastal Flood Advisory was put in place starting December 31 through January 5, initially for the San Francisco and Monterey Bays but eventually including the Pacific Coast. A Coastal Flood Warning was put in place for January 2 and 3 for the San Francisco and San Pablo Bayshore areas. The San Francisco tide gauge reached 8.40 feet above MLLW on January 3, which would make it the fourth highest water level at the station on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal Flooding was reported at the onramp from Sausalito to northbound US Highway 101. The Marin City onramp to the northbound lanes was also completely flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russian River exceeded flood stage at the Hopland Gauge around noon on the 5th causing flooding of Highway 175 and a full closure of the road east of Hopland. The river fell below flood stage during the morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with storm surge based off westerly swell and strong winds contributed to major coastal flooding impacts during the first few days of January. Impacts were seen across the coastal regions of the Bay Area and Central Coast, including locations of the Pacific coast that were typically insulated from the impacts of tidal flooding. A Coastal Flood Advisory was put in place starting December 31 through January 5, initially for the San Francisco and Monterey Bays but eventually including the Pacific Coast. A Coastal Flood Warning was put in place for January 2 and 3 for the San Francisco and San Pablo Bayshore areas. The San Francisco tide gauge reached 8.40 feet above MLLW on January 3, which would make it the fourth highest water level at the station on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eastbound lane of Tiburon Boulevard blocked with water at Blackfield Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms impacted the Bay Area and Central Coast from January 1st to the 5th, causing numerous flooding impacts and scattered thunderstorms. A low pressure system on the 1st was followed by a strong cold front and associated atmospheric river late on the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd.  A second cold front followed on the 4th. A tornado warning was issued on January 5 for the Pacific coast near the border between San Mateo and Santa Cruz counties due to a radar indicated rotation signature. Around 3,400 customers in San Francisco lost power on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound lane of Wilson Road was flooded at Shiloh Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crash was reported at 7:30 am on January 30th, 2026. A vehicle traveling at an unknown speed went through a stop sign and over an embankment into the Sacramento River. Foggy conditions were reported at the time of the incident. Observations at the time of the incident were a quarter mile or less. This incident resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A crash was reported near Walnut Grove, CA in Sacramento County at intersection of Twin Cities Rd and River Rd around 7:30 AM on January 30th. Dense fog was being observed at the time. Fairfield visibility observations were down to 0.12 miles, Davis Airport was down to less than a quarter mile, Sacramento Executive Airport was down to 1 mile, and Stockton Airport was down to a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern brought rounds of rain, mountain snow and gusty southerly winds from January 1st through January 5th. There were numerous flooding reports from moderate to heavy rain, along with some reports of mud and rock slides in the foothills. Ski resorts, Caltrans and the UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 1 to 4 feet of heavy snow in the mountains. There was one fatality of a hiker who had gone missing with these storms. Gusty southerly winds of 35 to 50 mph were also observed at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at River Road and Ord Ferry Road from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive long lasting period of foggy conditions prevailed across the San Joaquin Valley from January 12th through January 31st (It continued through February 7th). Dense fog coverage increased for the next several days each morning starting on the 11th. A localized area with near zero visibility was reported south of Fresno on the morning (around 9:15 AM) of the 11th that unfortunately resulted in a multi-vehicle collision that caused quite a few injuries and one fatality. Fog continued to develop each night and morning for at least another week afterward with some locations remaining foggy for the entire day during the 16th until the 19th, or only a brief improvement in visibility during the afternoon hours. ||On the 20th and 21st, most locations in the Central Valley continued to observe fog during the nighttime and morning hours, but was not as widespread. Also, valley fog onset was largely later in the evening on the 20th. The fog pattern continued through the end of the month, except most locations in the San Joaquin Valley did not observe dense fog on the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th due to an upper-level disturbance (with the exception of Merced). The fog continued through the end of the month. However, more major accidents due to fog happened on the 27th and 31st with numerous injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station KFCH Fresno Chandler Executive Airport reported less than one quarter-mile visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong Santa Ana winds set up late in the day on January 8th along coastal slopes and into the valleys of Southern California in the wake of an inside slider low pressure system. The pattern remained favorable for Santa Ana winds through the weekend with strong surface high pressure in place over the intermountain west. Gusts of 60-80+ mph were common along coastal slopes and through the foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Edison mesonet station recorded a 89 mph wind gust at Cloudland Cutoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive long lasting period of foggy conditions prevailed across the San Joaquin Valley from January 12th through January 31st (It continued through February 7th). Dense fog coverage increased for the next several days each morning starting on the 11th. A localized area with near zero visibility was reported south of Fresno on the morning (around 9:15 AM) of the 11th that unfortunately resulted in a multi-vehicle collision that caused quite a few injuries and one fatality. CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong Santa Ana winds set up late in the day on January 8th along coastal slopes and into the valleys of Southern California in the wake of an inside slider low pressure system. The pattern remained favorable for Santa Ana winds through the weekend with strong surface high pressure in place over the intermountain west. Gusts of 60-80+ mph were common along coastal slopes and through the foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Edison mesonet station recorded a 65 mph wind gust on Sierra Ave north of Fontana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the region from the 3rd to the 5th of January. This produced between 1 to 2 feet of snow in the Tahoe Basin with 2 to 4 feet for the higher elevations. This system also produced winds upwards of 90 mph on the ridges, with gusts commonly upwards of 55 mph elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHs station CA-PM-16 Portola measured 4 inches of snow from the 3rd to 5th. Also, CoCoRaHs station CA-LS-1 Susanville 3.2 SSW measured 0.5 inches of snow in the same period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry weather pattern with light winds and clear skies resulted in very cold temperatures across the region. Low temperatures dropped to about 3-5 degrees below freezing in the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 27 and 32 degrees each day between January 10 and January 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern brought rounds of rain, mountain snow and gusty southerly winds from January 1st through January 5th. There were numerous flooding reports from moderate to heavy rain, along with some reports of mud and rock slides in the foothills. Ski resorts, Caltrans and the UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 1 to 4 feet of heavy snow in the mountains. There was one fatality of a hiker who had gone missing with these storms. Gusty southerly winds of 35 to 50 mph were also observed at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at State Route 4 and Wilson Way from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 3rd-5th, rain and mountain snow fell due to atmospheric rivers passing over the region. On the 3rd, precipitation reached near one inch in the San Joaquin Valley, mainly southern portions. Other valley locations received around 0.25 inch to just over 0.50 inch. Lesser amounts occurred in the Central Valley during the 4th-5th. Several inches up to around one foot of snow fell to around 6,000 feet during this period, with heavier mounts above 8,000 feet, or around 1-2 feet. Mainly clear skies and cool temperatures prevailed afterward, including on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gran Grove COOP station picked up an estimated 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive long lasting period of foggy conditions prevailed across the San Joaquin Valley from January 12th through January 31st (It continued through February 7th). Dense fog coverage increased for the next several days each morning starting on the 11th. A localized area with near zero visibility was reported south of Fresno on the morning (around 9:15 AM) of the 11th that unfortunately resulted in a multi-vehicle collision that caused quite a few injuries and one fatality. Fog continued to develop each night and morning for at least another week afterward with some locations remaining foggy for the entire day during the 16th until the 19th, or only a brief improvement in visibility during the afternoon hours. ||On the 20th and 21st, most locations in the Central Valley continued to observe fog during the nighttime and morning hours, but was not as widespread. Also, valley fog onset was largely later in the evening on the 20th. The fog pattern continued through the end of the month, except most locations in the San Joaquin Valley did not observe dense fog on the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th due to an upper-level disturbance (with the exception of Merced). The fog continued through the end of the month. However, more major accidents due to fog happened on the 27th and 31st with numerous injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility as low as 200-300 feet along 6th Ave in Kings County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the region from the 3rd to the 5th of January. This produced between 1 to 2 feet of snow in the Tahoe Basin with 2 to 4 feet for the higher elevations. This system also produced winds upwards of 90 mph on the ridges, with gusts commonly upwards of 55 mph elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 40.4 inches of snowfall and CO-OP observer at Tahoe City measured 11.5 inches of snow. This was from the 3rd to the 5th. In addition,|Northstar Ski Base reported 29 inches, Palisades Tahoe Ski Base reported 17 inches, and CoCoRaHs station CA-NV-51 Truckee 1.6 N reported 18.3 inches. These were storm totals for the event from the 3rd to 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the southwest tapped into anomalously moist tropical air as it pushed into Southern California on New Year's Day. This allowed for some heavy rainfall rates and flooding, especially near the San Diego Metro. There were several reports of flooding along the San Diego River. Showers continued off and on in the following days, but impacts were limited after New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 2.20 of rain over a 2 hour period. At 6:00 AM the storm total was 0.59 inch. At 8:00 AM PST it is now 2.79 inch and continuing to rise from steady heavy showers.

Fog continued to develop each night and morning for at least another week afterward with some locations remaining foggy for the entire day during the 16th until the 19th, or only a brief improvement in visibility during the afternoon hours. ||On the 20th and 21st, most locations in the Central Valley continued to observe fog during the nighttime and morning hours, but was not as widespread. Also, valley fog onset was largely later in the evening on the 20th. The fog pattern continued through the end of the month, except most locations in the San Joaquin Valley did not observe dense fog on the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th due to an upper-level disturbance (with the exception of Merced). The fog continued through the end of the month. However, more major accidents due to fog happened on the 27th and 31st with numerous injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense Fog, visibility between 500 and 250 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm systems continued to bring rain to Northwest California in early January. Persistent south winds resulted in over 1.5 feet of storm surge along the coast resulting in inundation of many homes in the King Salmon area. The south winds also caused isolated power outages and a few downed trees. Flooding was generally restricted to the typical flooding locations around Humboldt Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly every street flooded in the community of King Salmon. Two businesses and two homes experienced flooding during the highest tide. The high tide reached a record of 10.01 FT MLLW at the North Spit Tide Gauge at 9:54 am. This exceeded the old record of 9.98 FT MLLW set on December 31st, 2005. Flooding also reported on secondary roads in Eureka, along the waterfront onto grassy areas and the coastal trail, and in the Arcata Bottom over Jackson Ranch Road. Water was also seen flowing between storm drains along the side of Highway 101 through Eureka near the intersection with Broadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadways blocked by trees at the Garberville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm systems continued to bring rain to Northwest California in early January. Persistent south winds resulted in over 1.5 feet of storm surge along the coast resulting in inundation of many homes in the King Salmon area. The south winds also caused isolated power outages and a few downed trees. Flooding was generally restricted to the typical flooding locations around Humboldt Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New record high tide measured at the North Spit Tide Gauge of 10.37 FT MLLW at 11 am, exceeding the record stage measured the day before of 10.01 FT MLLW. Impacts in the Humboldt Bay area were extreme with over 40 homes, 2 businesses, and multiple cars flooded in King Salmon. All roads in King Salmon were flooded. Jackson Ranch Road in the Arcata Bottom was flooded and closed. Water was reported on numerous secondary streets in the Eureka area including the waterfront parking lots and roads in Old Town near the Jack's Seafood restaurant. Water crossed Highway 255 near the Eureka Slough and reached the side of Highway 101 on Highway 101 near Tompkins Hill Road. Flooding also closed the tunnel on Woodley Island, flooded Vance Avenue in Samoa, flooded New Navy Base Road near the RV park, flooded Pine Hill Road at the intersection of Elk River Road, flooded Cannibal Island Road in the Eel River delta, flooded Camp Weott Road, and flooded the Humboldt Bay trail extensively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm systems continued to bring rain to Northwest California in early January. Persistent south winds resulted in over 1.5 feet of storm surge along the coast resulting in inundation of many homes in the King Salmon area. The south winds also caused isolated power outages and a few downed trees. Flooding was generally restricted to the typical flooding locations around Humboldt Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding around Humboldt Bay continues for a third day at high tide. Highest tide exceeded 9.9 ft MLLW and caused flooding in all the typical locations around Humboldt Bay included roads and one business in King Salmon, Pine Hill Road at the Elk River, some secondary roads around Eureka, Cannibal Island Road, Vance Avenue in Samoa, and Jackson Ranch Road in the Arcata Bottom.
